title: F5 BIG-IP APM Pre-Auth RCE (CVE-2025-53521) Added to CISA KEV — Six-Month-Old DoS Flaw Reclassified as Critical Remote Code Execution Under Active Exploitation

sources.name: Help Net Security - Attackers are exploiting RCE vulnerability in BIG-IP APM systems
UK NCSC - Vulnerability affecting F5 BIG-IP APM
BleepingComputer - Hackers now exploit critical F5 BIG-IP flaw in attacks
The Hacker News - CISA Adds CVE-2025-53521 to KEV After Active F5 BIG-IP APM Exploitation
CISA - Adds One Known Exploited Vulnerability to Catalog (March 28)
Cyber Daily - CISA, experts concerned over active exploitation of F5 BIG-IP APM vulnerability
Cyberpress - CISA Alerts on Actively Exploited F5 BIG-IP Flaw Targeting Organizations
VPN Central - CISA flags actively exploited F5 BIG-IP flaw as agencies race to patch
Rankiteo - CISA experts concerned over active exploitation of F5 BIG-IP APM vulnerability

timeline.event: F5 originally discloses CVE-2025-53521 as a denial-of-service vulnerability in BIG-IP APM with CVSS 7.5 and releases patches across multiple branches
New research reveals CVE-2025-53521 is actually a pre-authentication remote code execution vulnerability; F5 reclassifies severity to CVSS 9.8 (v3.1) and 9.3 (v4.0)
CISA adds CVE-2025-53521 to KEV catalog citing evidence of active exploitation; UK NCSC issues parallel advisory; federal agencies given deadline of March 30 to patch
Multiple security firms confirm widespread exploitation attempts targeting unpatched BIG-IP APM instances; organizations that treated this as a low-priority DoS fix face urgent remediation

simple_summary.what_happened: A vulnerability in F5 BIG-IP Access Policy Manager (APM) that was originally reported in October 2025 as a moderate denial-of-service issue has been reclassified as a critical pre-authentication remote code execution vulnerability. The CVSS score has been upgraded from 7.5 to 9.8, reflecting the true severity. F5 confirmed that unauthenticated remote attackers can execute arbitrary code on affected BIG-IP APM systems without any credentials. CISA added this vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on March 28, 2026 after confirming active exploitation in the wild, giving federal agencies an emergency deadline of March 30 to apply patches.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization running F5 BIG-IP APM versions 15.1.0 through 15.1.10, 16.1.0 through 16.1.6, 17.1.0 through 17.1.2, or 17.5.0 through 17.5.1 with an APM access policy configured on a virtual server. BIG-IP APM is widely deployed in large enterprises, government agencies, healthcare networks, and financial institutions for VPN access, application access control, and identity federation. Organizations that deprioritized this patch when it was classified as a DoS issue in October 2025 are now urgently exposed.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers scan for internet-facing F5 BIG-IP APM instances using service fingerprinting. Step 2: They send crafted traffic to virtual servers with APM access policies, achieving unauthenticated remote code execution. Step 3: With code execution on the BIG-IP appliance, attackers gain access to the network perimeter device and can intercept traffic, steal credentials, and pivot into internal networks.
what_you_should_do: Apply F5 patches immediately — the fixes released in October 2025 for the DoS classification remain effective for the RCE; upgrade to BIG-IP 17.5.2+, 17.1.2.1+, 16.1.6.1+, or 15.1.10.1+
If patching cannot be done immediately, restrict access to the APM virtual server to trusted networks only using F5 iRules or external firewall rules
Audit BIG-IP system logs for signs of compromise since March 26, 2026 when the RCE nature was publicly disclosed; look for unexpected processes, modified files, or new user accounts
Review network traffic logs for unusual outbound connections from BIG-IP appliances that could indicate post-compromise activity
Verify that all BIG-IP APM instances are inventoried — shadow IT or forgotten test instances are particularly at risk if they were skipped during the October 2025 patch cycle
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This vulnerability is exceptionally dangerous because it was initially classified as a moderate denial-of-service issue, meaning many organizations likely deprioritized patching when it was first disclosed in October 2025. The reclassification to a pre-authentication RCE six months later means that unpatched systems have been unknowingly exposed to a critical vulnerability. BIG-IP APM appliances sit at the network perimeter and handle VPN access and authentication — compromising one gives attackers a foothold at the most sensitive point in the network. The CISA KEV addition with an emergency deadline of March 30 (already passed) underscores the urgency for any remaining unpatched systems.

tactics.description: Unauthenticated remote code execution on internet-facing F5 BIG-IP APM virtual servers with access policies configured
Crafted network traffic triggers code execution on the BIG-IP APM appliance without requiring any authentication
Compromised BIG-IP APM appliance can intercept VPN and authentication traffic passing through the device
Attackers use compromised BIG-IP appliance as a pivot point to access internal network services

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on identifying unpatched BIG-IP APM instances, monitoring for unusual process execution and network traffic from BIG-IP appliances, and auditing system integrity. Organizations should treat any unpatched BIG-IP APM as potentially compromised given the extended exposure window.

log_sources.description: Monitor for unusual process creation, unexpected restarts, error messages indicating exploitation attempts, and authentication anomalies in APM logs
Monitor outbound connections from BIG-IP management interfaces and data plane IPs for unexpected destinations, especially to known C2 infrastructure
Compare current BIG-IP configuration and filesystem against known-good baselines to detect unauthorized modifications, new user accounts, or injected iRules
Run authenticated vulnerability scans against all BIG-IP instances to verify patch status across the environment; flag any instance below fixed versions

what_to_look_for.description: Any unpatched BIG-IP APM instance with access policies is vulnerable to unauthenticated RCE and should be treated as a critical finding

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected processes running on BIG-IP appliance
what_to_look_for.description: Post-compromise activity may include unfamiliar processes, modified system binaries, or new cron jobs on the BIG-IP system
what_to_look_for.log_field: process.name NOT IN known_bigip_processes AND host_type='bigip'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unusual outbound network connections from BIG-IP
what_to_look_for.description: Compromised BIG-IP appliances may establish outbound connections to C2 servers; legitimate BIG-IP should have limited outbound connectivity
what_to_look_for.log_field: src_ip IN bigip_management_ips AND dst_ip NOT IN approved_destinations

what_to_look_for.indicator: New or modified user accounts on BIG-IP
what_to_look_for.description: Attackers may create backdoor accounts on compromised appliances for persistent access

immediate_actions.action: Patch all BIG-IP APM instances immediately
immediate_actions.description: Apply F5 patches released in October 2025: upgrade to 17.5.2+, 17.1.2.1+, 16.1.6.1+, or 15.1.10.1+ to remediate CVE-2025-53521

immediate_actions.action: Inventory all BIG-IP APM instances including shadow IT
immediate_actions.description: Ensure every BIG-IP APM instance in the environment is accounted for — forgotten test or dev instances are at highest risk if missed during the October 2025 patch cycle

immediate_actions.action: Restrict APM virtual server access as interim mitigation
immediate_actions.description: If immediate patching is not possible, restrict access to APM virtual servers to trusted IP ranges using firewall rules or F5 iRules

immediate_actions.action: Audit BIG-IP appliances for signs of compromise
immediate_actions.description: Check for unauthorized user accounts, modified system files, unexpected processes, and unusual outbound network connections from all BIG-IP appliances
immediate_actions.command: tmsh list auth user; ls -la /var/log/; ps aux | grep -v tmm

immediate_actions.description: Review network logs for connections originating from BIG-IP management IPs to internal systems that could indicate attackers using the appliance as a pivot point
